title: How Hannibal’s War Elephants Helped to Determine His Route Through the Alps
gist: An Oxford/Friedrich Schiller University PNAS study uses elephant bioenergetics to reconstruct Hannibal's 218 B.C. Alpine crossing, arguing that feeding 37 war elephants forced his army onto the shortest viable route.

insights.claim: In 218 B.C. Hannibal crossed the Alps with an estimated 40,000 soldiers, 7,000 horses, and 37 war elephants used as heavy cavalry to carry supplies and confront enemy lines.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Anchors the scale and logistics of the campaign.

insights.claim: A PNAS study by Oxford and Friedrich Schiller University Jena reconstructs Hannibal's most likely Alpine path by analyzing the bioenergetics of the crossing, borrowing a modeling approach from studies of African elephants.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Shows animal energetics can resolve centuries-old historical debates.

insights.claim: The energy demands of feeding nearly 40 elephants over a 15-day crossing imply the army had to take the shortest possible path through the mountains.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Constraint-based reasoning narrows candidate routes debated for centuries.
